frameworks.name: discrete attractor dynamics
frameworks.one_line: stable neuronal activity configurations as candidate substrate for working memory

frameworks.name: attractor dynamics with activity-dependent plasticity
frameworks.one_line: discrete attractors augmented with short-term plasticity to span multiple delays

core_question: Can discrete attractor dynamics account for human working memory across multiple delay timescales?
key_assumptions: working memories can be stored in a discrete set of stable activity configurations
single delay-period responses reflect attractor occupancy
longer delays carry more stimulus information than discrete states allow
manipulations: delay interval length across multiple timescales
model class: discrete attractor vs attractor + activity-dependent plasticity
key_findings: discrete attractor dynamics approximate working memory at a single timescale
discrete attractor models fail to generalize across multiple delay timescales
responses at longer delays contain more stimulus information than a discrete attractor can store
combined attractor + activity-dependent plasticity model generalizes across timescales and predicts intertrial interactions
interpretations: discrete attractor dynamics alone are insufficient to model human working memory
activity-dependent plasticity improves durability of information storage in attractor systems
